

**1\. Summary**  
Advertisers on paid social platforms face a recurring challenge: their internal audience definitions — rich psychographic personas, brand briefs, and customer descriptions — do not map cleanly to the interest taxonomies and audience segments exposed by social channel APIs. The result is imprecise targeting, wasted spend, and audiences that approximate rather than match true customer intent.

This project proposes building an AI-powered audience matching solution that bridges the gap. By embedding both the brand’s audience description and the social platform’s available targeting options into a shared semantic vector space, the system can rank the best-fit audience segments via cosine similarity — then surface audience size estimates to make those recommendations immediately actionable.

**2\. Proposed Solution**

The solution consists of three integrated components, with an optional fourth extension for brand-specific persona libraries.

**2.1  Audience Discovery via Platform APIs**

At the foundation, the system ingests available audience segments directly from each social channel’s Targeting API. This ensures coverage is always current and complete.

Meta Targeting API — key endpoints:

* GET /act\_{ad\_account\_id}/targetingbrowse — browses the full interest taxonomy (6M+ interests organized into categories)  
* GET /act\_{ad\_account\_id}/targetingsearch?q=fitness — searches interests by keyword and returns estimated audience sizes  
* GET /act\_{ad\_account\_id}/targetingsuggestions — given a seed interest, returns related ones


Snapchat Targeting API — interest segment example:

| GET https://adsapi.snapchat.com/v1/targeting/interests/scls?country\_code=us   Authorization: Bearer {token} Response example:   { "id": "SLC\_36", "name": "Foodies",     "path": "/Food & Drink/Foodies",     "parent\_id": "SLC\_35", "source": "SNAPCHAT",     "is\_hec": false   // is\_hec flags High Engagement Content segments } |
| :---- |

**2.2  Semantic Embedding & Cosine Similarity Matching**

The core matching engine uses text embedding models to encode both the brand’s input and the platform’s audience segments into a shared vector space. Matching then becomes a nearest-neighbour search.

The workflow is as follows:

* The user inputs a customer description, persona, or set of keywords (e.g. a lifestyle profile or brand brief).

* The input is passed through an embedding model to produce a dense vector representation.

* All available audience segment names and descriptions from the target platform are pre-embedded and stored in a vector index (depending on the response time requirements, we might not need a vector index since it’s a 1→N matching, not N→N)

* Cosine similarity is computed between each input embedding and every segment embedding.

* Results are ranked by the combined similarity score; this can be either 1\) Simple average, 2\) Weighted average based on user input, 3\) Sum of squared distances between each embedding, 4\) cosine similarity in combination with filter on specific category similarity

* Keep the top N ranks, based on final audience size estimates

This approach has been validated in adjacent contexts — for example, embedding 1,000 domains and apps from The Trade Desk and matching them against brand and category inputs — with strong relevance results. The same architecture applies directly to social interest taxonomies.

![][image1]

	

Specific example using some Meta interest segments:

![][image2]

**2.3  Alternative approaches**

Extract keywords and themes from input text and use audience matching on either the DSP or social channel side to build the audience.  The way this would work is that the keywords sent to the DSP \+ social channels would be a combination of the extracted keywords from the text and themes determined for the text

Example:

Keyword extraction:

![][image3]

Theme extraction:

![][image4]

Output: \['Family', 'Pet Owners', 'Travel Enthusiasts'\]

As we can see the theme extraction can be more accurate than extracting the keywords

**2.4  Audience Size Estimation**

Where the platform API supports it, each matched segment will be annotated with an estimated audience size. This allows the system to surface not just the most semantically relevant segments but also their reach implications.

Proposed outputs include:

* Segment-level reach estimates (where available from the platform API).

* Cumulative reach estimates for the top 1%, 2%, and 5% of matched segments (see section 2.2)

* Overlap warnings where selected segments are likely to compete for the same users.

**2.5  Pre-built Persona Library (Extension)**

To accelerate audience setup for planners who do not have a detailed brief, the system will include a curated library of pre-built lifestyle persona descriptions. These are designed to span a diverse range of demographics, psychographics, and purchase motivations, and can be customized per brand.

Twenty initial profiles are documented in Section 5\. Each profile includes a psychographic description and a set of indicative brand examples to calibrate the tone and category focus.

**3\. Platform Coverage**

The initial release will target the following platforms, prioritized by API maturity and advertiser demand:

| Platform | API Maturity | Interest Segments | Size Estimates |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Meta | High — full taxonomy API | 6M+ interests | Yes — per keyword |
| Snapchat | High — documented REST API | 1,000+ segments | Yes — range estimates |
| TTD |  |  |  |
| Yahoo DSP |  |  |  |

**4\. Proposed Roadmap**

The project is proposed in four sequential phases over approximately 16 weeks.

| Phase | Title | Duration | Key Deliverables |
| ----- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Phase 1** | **API Integration & Data Pipeline** | Weeks 1–3 | Connect to Meta, Snapchat, Yahoo, \+  Ingest and normalize interest taxonomy data  |
| **Phase 2** | **Embedding Engine & Matching Core** | Weeks 4–8 | Select and evaluate embedding model (OpenAI, Gemini, or open-source…probably open source will be good enough) *Stretch: train contextual similarity model* Build vector index for all platform segments Implement cosine similarity ranking pipeline Validate matching quality against manual benchmarks |
| **Phase 3** | **Audience Size Integration & Scoring** | Weeks 9–12 | Integrate audience size estimates per platform Build cumulative reach calculator for top-N matches Develop confidence scoring for match quality Determine optimal approach for determining top N segments |
| **Phase 4** | **Persona Library & Product Surface** | Weeks 13–16 | Build pre-built persona library (20+ profiles, brand-customizable) Design and implement user-facing interface End-to-end testing across all platforms Pilot with 2–3 brand clients and iterate |

**5\. Open Questions & Risks**

The following items require resolution before or during Phase 1:

* **Which model provides the best balance of semantic quality, cost, and latency for this use case?** 

* **Time to develop a custom contextual mapping?**

* **What are the risks associated with showing results that don’t seem contextually similar?  Can this be mitigated by allowing the user to make the final selection of what is used to build the audience**

* **Ground-truth labeling for match quality assessment — who evaluates, at what scale, and what is an acceptable false positive rate**

**Appendix: Pre-built Lifestyle Persona Library**

The following 20 profiles form the initial pre-built persona library. Each is designed to be immediately usable as an embedding input for audience matching, and can be extended or customized on a per-brand basis.  This is potentially a fallback solution to constrain the matched output

| 1 | Quiet Achievers | CAREER |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Mid-career professionals (30–45) who prioritize steady advancement over hustle culture. They value quality over flash — premium but understated. Heavy podcast listeners; buy tools that save time and signal competence. **Relevant brands:**  *Moleskine, Calm app, Patagonia, Allbirds, Audible* |  |

| 2 | Outdoor Minimalists | LIFESTYLE |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Trail runners, climbers, and backpackers who distrust brand noise. Gear-obsessed but only for function. They research deeply before buying and are skeptical of greenwashing. Influential in peer communities. **Relevant brands:**  *Arc’teryx, Osprey, Garmin, Nuun, REI* |  |

| 3 | Health Optimizers | WELLNESS |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Biohackers and fitness-focused individuals tracking everything: sleep, macros, HRV, glucose. Early adopters of wearables and supplements. Respond to data and science-backed claims, not aspirational imagery. **Relevant brands:**  *Whoop, Eight Sleep, AG1, Levels, Thorne* |  |

| 4 | Conscious Parents | FAMILY |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Urban and suburban parents aged 30–42 making ingredient-conscious purchases for their children. Willing to pay premiums for clean formulas. Research-driven; community-influenced (mom Facebook groups, Reddit). **Relevant brands:**  *Honest Company, Thrive Market, Lovevery, Little Spoon* |  |

| 5 | Digital Nomads | TRAVEL |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Location-independent workers who travel full-time or seasonally. Infrastructure-dependent: they prioritize wifi, portable gear, and banking flexibility. Aspirational to millions but a small, influential actual segment. **Relevant brands:**  *Wise, Airbnb, Away luggage, Bose, Notion* |  |

| 6 | Aesthetic Renters | HOME |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Young renters (22–34) investing heavily in home aesthetics despite not owning. Instagram and Pinterest-driven. Buy décor, plants, and lighting to curate their environment. High fashion sensitivity on a budget. **Relevant brands:**  *IKEA, Artifact Uprising, Etsy, Canopy humidifier, Ruggable* |  |

| 7 | Side Hustle Builders | ENTREPRENEURSHIP |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Full-time employees running or building a secondary income stream — freelancing, reselling, content creation, or e-commerce. Time-starved and tool-hungry. Respond to ROI framing and efficiency narratives. **Relevant brands:**  *Shopify, Canva, QuickBooks, ConvertKit, Fiverr* |  |

| 8 | Silver Adventurers | TRAVEL |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Empty nesters (55–70) with high disposable income and time. They travel frequently and well, seek authentic experiences over resort packages. More digitally fluent than brands assume. Loyalty-driven. **Relevant brands:**  *Viking Cruises, Merino Wool brands, Bose, Nikon, Audible* |  |

| 9 | Gen Z Thrifters | SUSTAINABILITY |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Ages 18–26 who shop secondhand, upcycle, and reject fast fashion philosophically while navigating tight budgets. Brand-skeptical but community-loyal. TikTok and Discord are primary discovery channels. **Relevant brands:**  *Depop, Vinted, Nuuly, Allbirds, Patagonia Worn Wear* |  |

| 10 | Urban Pet Parents | LIFESTYLE |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | City-dwelling millennials who treat pets as family members. High pet-category spend relative to income: premium food, vet insurance, stylish accessories. Emotional buyers; deeply engaged with pet influencer content. **Relevant brands:**  *Ollie, Trupanion, Ruffwear, Yeti pet gear, BarkBox* |  |

| 11 | Kitchen Obsessives | FOOD |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Home cooks who invest in high-quality cookware, specialty ingredients, and cooking education. Not professional chefs — they cook for pleasure, creativity, and entertaining. Recipe content and YouTube-driven. **Relevant brands:**  *Made In Cookware, Brightland oil, Eataly, Salt & Straw, MasterClass* |  |

| 12 | Frugal Maximizers | FINANCE |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Personal finance enthusiasts who obsessively optimize spend, rewards points, and savings rates. Podcast listeners (ChooseFI, Planet Money). High CLV for financial products; influence their social circles heavily. **Relevant brands:**  *Chase Sapphire, Fidelity, Rocket Money, Monarch, YNAB* |  |

| 13 | Luxury Minimalists | FASHION |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Fashion-conscious professionals who buy fewer but better things. Anti-logo, quality-fabric buyers. Spend on heritage brands and direct-to-consumer elevated basics. Long decision cycles with near-zero returns. **Relevant brands:**  *Cuyana, Everlane, A.P.C., Aesop, Polène* |  |

| 14 | Weekend Warriors | FITNESS |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Recreational athletes (cycling, golf, tennis, skiing) who compete socially and invest seriously in their hobby 2–3 days a week. High gear spend in a single category. Status-aware within their sport community. **Relevant brands:**  *Specialized, Titleist, Lululemon, GoPro, Theragun* |  |

| 15 | Cultural Explorers | ENTERTAINMENT |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Arts and culture enthusiasts who attend theater, visit museums, read widely, and prioritize cultural experiences over material goods. Often educators or creatives. Respond to mission-driven and craft-forward brand stories. **Relevant brands:**  *Criterion Collection, Headspace, Tatine candles, independent bookstores* |  |

| 16 | Tech Early Adopters | TECHNOLOGY |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Consumers who buy new gadgets in the first weeks of launch, write reviews, and shape peer adoption. Follow tech media obsessively. Moderate-to-high income; derive identity from being ahead of the curve. **Relevant brands:**  *Apple, Samsung Galaxy, DJI, Sonos, Nothing Phone* |  |

| 17 | Healing Seekers | WELLNESS |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | People actively managing stress, burnout, trauma, or chronic illness through a layered approach: therapy, supplements, movement, and community. Skeptical of hype but hungry for legitimate solutions. Spend without ceiling when trust is built. **Relevant brands:**  *Headspace, Cerebral, Seed probiotics, Oura, Othership* |  |

| 18 | New Suburbanites | HOME |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Young families who recently moved to suburbs from cities (25–38). First-time homeowners navigating lawn care, renovations, and a car-dependent lifestyle. Overwhelmed but aspirational; heavy YouTube and Houzz users. **Relevant brands:**  *Home Depot, Sunday lawn care, SimpliSafe, Costco, Weber* |  |

| 19 | Quiet Luxury Devotees | FASHION |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Income-flexible consumers (28–50) buying into understated wealth signaling: neutral palettes, cashmere, fine leather. Aspirational positioning is core to purchase. Influenced heavily by editorial content and celebrity paparazzi style. **Relevant brands:**  *Bottega Veneta, The Row, Loro Piana, Brunello Cucinelli, Toteme* |  |

| 20 | Community Anchors | SOCIAL |
| ----- | :---- | ----: |
|  | Individuals for whom local community is a core identity — volunteer organizers, neighborhood association members, small business patrons. Trust peer and local recommendations over national advertising. High word-of-mouth influence. **Relevant brands:**  *Local credit unions, Nextdoor, farmers markets, small-batch CPG brands* |  |
